title: Custom exception classes

overview.what: Custom exceptions are your own error classes created by inheriting from Python's built-in `Exception` class. By convention, their names end in `Error` (e.g. `UserNotFoundError`). You can add custom attributes to them, such as `status_code` or `user_id`, allowing the exception to carry structured data about the failure up to the caller. A common professional pattern is to create one base exception for your application (`class MyAppError(Exception): pass`), and have all specific errors inherit from it.
overview.why: Built-in exceptions like `ValueError` are too generic. If your service catches a `ValueError`, did it happen because a user provided bad input, or because a database configuration was malformed? Custom exceptions create a domain-specific vocabulary. By raising `InsufficientFundsError`, the caller can catch EXACTLY that error and trigger a specific UI flow, without accidentally catching unrelated standard library errors.
where_used: Building SDKs so users can catch specific API errors
Domain-driven design to express business logic failures
Mapping specific exceptions to HTTP 400/404/500 responses in FastAPI

why_learning_this: Creating a clear error hierarchy for your application
Attaching context (like an HTTP status code or a missing ID) directly to the error object
Allowing callers to catch specific business errors while letting catastrophic system errors crash

common_mistakes.title: Inheriting from BaseException
common_mistakes.explanation: You should always inherit from `Exception`. Inheriting directly from `BaseException` means your custom error won't be caught by a standard `except Exception:` block, which usually breaks assumptions made by third-party frameworks.

common_mistakes.title: Forgetting to call super().__init__
common_mistakes.explanation: If you define a custom `__init__` to accept extra arguments (like `status_code`), you must remember to call `super().__init__(message)` so the base `Exception` class can properly set up the traceback and string representation.

recall_questions.q: When creating a custom exception, which built-in class should you inherit from?
recall_questions.answer: You should inherit from `Exception` (or a more specific subclass of it like `ValueError`), never from `BaseException`.

recall_questions.q: What is the primary architectural benefit of creating a base exception class for your entire application (e.g. `class MyAppError(Exception): pass`)?
recall_questions.answer: It allows a caller or framework to catch `MyAppError` to handle all domain-specific errors generated by your code, while letting built-in system errors (like `TypeError` or `MemoryError`) pass through.

practice_tasks.title: Create a domain error
practice_tasks.prompt: Define a custom exception `UserNotFoundError` that inherits from `Exception`. Then write a function `get_user(user_id)` that raises this exception with the message 'User 404 not found' if `user_id` is 404. Otherwise, return 'Success'.

code_walkthrough.focus: The custom `__init__` allows the exception to carry a specific `code` attribute, turning the error into a structured data carrier.

challenge.title: Build a custom validation error
challenge.prompt: Create an `InvalidEmailError` that takes an invalid email string. Write `validate_email(email)` that raises it if there's no '@'. Catch it and print its message.
